# Macronutrients: carbohydrate, protein, fat and fibre

> *The energy-yielding nutrients — but the recurring lesson is that **quality**, not just
> quantity, drives health. Same kilocalories can mean very different physiology.*

## The three macronutrients (and fibre)

Macronutrients are the nutrients we need in **gram quantities** and the ones that supply
**energy**. There are three — carbohydrate, protein and fat — plus **dietary fibre**, a
carbohydrate we mostly *don't* digest but which earns its own place. Their energy yields:

| Macronutrient | Energy (kcal/g) | Headline role |
|---|---|---|
| Carbohydrate | ~4 | Primary, glucose-yielding fuel |
| Protein | ~4 | Structure, enzymes, immune & transport functions |
| Fat | ~9 | Energy store, cell membranes, essential fatty acids |
| Fibre | ~2 | Fermented by gut microbes; not a fuel in the usual sense |
| *(Alcohol)* | *~7* | *Not a nutrient — empty energy, shown for contrast* |

Fat is the most energy-dense, which is why fat-rich foods raise total intake quickly. But
energy density is only part of the story. The question that matters clinically is almost
never *"how many grams?"* — it is *"what kind, from what food?"*

::: {.callout-important}
## The one idea to carry out of this chapter
"Are carbs bad? Is fat bad?" are **malformed questions**. The health effect of a
macronutrient depends on its **form and food source**, not a single good/bad label. Hold
this thought — we will return to it at the end.
:::

## Carbohydrate: a spectrum, not a single thing

Carbohydrates run a wide spectrum, and where a food sits on it matters far more than the
word "carb":

> **free sugars / refined starches  →  intact whole grains + fibre**

- **Free sugars** — added sugars plus those in syrups and fruit juices — are the
  carbohydrate to *limit*. The WHO caps them at **<10% of energy**, with further benefit
  below 5% [@who2020diet]. (Note: the natural sugar inside *whole* fruit is **not** a free
  sugar and is not the target.)
- **Refined starches** (white rice, white bread) are digested fast and stripped of fibre.
- **Whole grains and fibre** sit at the protective end. In a major series of meta-analyses,
  higher fibre and whole-grain intake was associated with roughly **15–30% lower**
  all-cause and cardiovascular mortality and lower rates of coronary disease, stroke, type
  2 diabetes and colorectal cancer — with benefit strengthening up to about **25–29 g of
  fibre a day** [@reynolds2019carb].

![Whatever their source, digestible carbohydrates are broken down to simple sugars — chiefly **glucose**, shown here in its ring form.](/figures/structures/beta-d-glucose.svg){#fig-glucose fig-alt="Skeletal ring structure of beta-D-glucose." width="230"}

::: {.callout-warning}
## How strong is the fibre evidence?
Mostly **prospective cohort** studies plus some trials — so the certainty is *moderate at
best* for fibre, and lower for whole grains and for glycaemic index/load. These are
**associations**, not proof of cause. Teach the benefit; keep the caveat attached.
:::

## Glycaemic index and glycaemic load

Not all carbohydrate raises blood glucose at the same speed. The **glycaemic index (GI)**
ranks a food by how much a portion containing **50 g of available carbohydrate** raises
blood glucose over about two hours, relative to pure glucose (set at **100**)
[@atkinson2021gitables]. Lower-GI foods give a gentler, slower rise:

- **Low GI:** ≤55 — most legumes, intact whole grains, most whole fruit, milk
- **Medium GI:** 56–69 — basmati rice, sweet potato
- **High GI:** ≥70 — white rice, white bread, glucose (= 100)

But GI has a blind spot: it fixes the carbohydrate at 50 g — a portion you may never
actually eat. **Glycaemic load (GL)** corrects for this by scaling GI to the *real* serving:

$$ \text{GL} = \frac{\text{GI} \times \text{available carbohydrate (g) per serving}}{100} $$

graded **low ≤10, medium 11–19, high ≥20** per serving. GL therefore captures *both* the
**quality** of the carbohydrate (its GI) and the **quantity** actually eaten.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The watermelon trick
Watermelon has a **high GI (~76)** — yet a normal slice holds only ~6 g of carbohydrate, so
its **GL is ~5 (low)**. A small portion of a high-GI food can be a low-load choice. The
reverse also holds: a *low*-GI noodle like *bee hoon* becomes a **high**-GL plate at hawker
portions. So "high GI = avoid" is the wrong shortcut — **both quality and portion matter.**
:::

Explore the relationship below. Pick a food — its GI sets the **height** of the glucose
curve — then drag the portion, which grows the **shaded area** (the glycaemic load). Switch
on **Compare** to see the watermelon-versus-potato paradox: near-identical peaks, very
different areas.

<div data-island="gi" data-src="/diagrams/glycemic-index-load.yml"></div>

A few honest caveats keep GI in proportion:

- GI is **not** a measure of healthiness — chocolate and ice cream are low-GI because of
  their fat, while watermelon and potato are high-GI. It describes only the glucose curve.
- A food has **no single fixed GI**: ripeness (a ripe banana raises glucose more than an
  unripe one), processing, cooking, and what it is eaten *with* (fat, protein, fibre and
  acidity all blunt the rise) all shift it. Table values are population averages.
- It is a **concept, not a bedside tool** at this stage — and the evidence linking GI/GL to
  hard health outcomes is the *weakest* strand of carbohydrate-quality research, rated
  **low to very low certainty** in the Reynolds meta-analyses, well below the evidence for
  fibre and whole grains [@reynolds2019carb].

For most patients the practical message is not a GI number but the familiar one: favour
**whole, intact, fibre-rich** carbohydrates in **sensible portions** — exactly what My
Healthy Plate's wholegrain quarter encodes [@hpbHealthyPlate].

::: {.callout-tip}
## Healthy kitchen: cooking changes carbohydrate
Cooking **gelatinises** starch, making it digest faster — so *al dente* pasta and firmer,
less-processed grains raise glucose less than the same food overcooked [@cai2021starchstructure].
Cook a starchy food and then **chill it**, and some starch *retrogrades* into **resistant
starch**, which acts partly like fibre: cooling cooked white rice for a day cut its
glycaemic response by ~18% in one small trial [@sonia2015rice]. Real, but modest — a
refinement of "quality over quantity," not a way to make white rice "low-carb."
:::

## Fat: type matters more than total

For decades the advice was "eat less fat." The better message is "eat **better** fat." Rank
fats by their relationship to cardiovascular risk:

> **trans fat  →  saturated  →  monounsaturated  →  polyunsaturated (incl. omega-3)**

- ***Trans* fat** is the clearest harm. WHO recommends **<1% of energy** and elimination of
  industrial sources [@who2020diet].
- **Saturated fat** raises LDL cholesterol; the practical move is to **replace it with
  unsaturated fat** — not with refined carbohydrate.
- **Mono- and polyunsaturated fats** (olive oil, nuts, fish) are the ones to favour;
  omega-3 and omega-6 are *essential* fatty acids the body cannot make.

![Nuts are a whole-food source of mono- and polyunsaturated fats. Photo: HaJunkiyada, CC BY-SA 4.0.](/figures/food/mixed-nuts.jpg){#fig-nuts fig-alt="An assortment of mixed nuts." width="320" .lightbox}

::: {.callout-note}
## "Low fat" can backfire
Chasing "low fat" often means swapping fat for refined starch or sugar — frequently a
*worse* trade. The target is fat **quality**, and the replacement nutrient matters.
*(Where saturated fat sits within a whole food — dairy versus processed meat — is an area
of genuine ongoing scientific debate; the first-year message stays simple: replace with
unsaturated and whole foods.)*
:::

### Seeing the difference: *cis*, *trans* and saturated

Oleic acid (olive oil) and elaidic acid (the classic industrial *trans* fat) contain the
**same atoms** — they differ only in the geometry of one double bond. Start with the **2D
formula**, then rotate the **3D** model, and add **stearic acid** (a saturated fat) for
comparison. A single **cis** bond kinks the chain, keeping unsaturated fats liquid; the
**trans** bond leaves the chain almost straight, so — like fully saturated stearic acid —
it packs tightly and behaves like a solid fat. The bend from one cis bond is modest;
polyunsaturated fats, with several cis bonds, are far more kinked. The shape *is* the
explanation.

<div data-island="molecule" data-src="/structures/dietary-fats.mol.yml"></div>

::: {.callout-tip}
## Healthy kitchen: oils and frying
Deep-frying draws oil *into* food as water boils out, so a fried version carries much more
fat and energy — and frequent fried-food intake tracks with higher type 2 diabetes and
heart-failure risk in cohort studies [@gadiraju2015fried]. Overheating oil past its smoke
point, or **reusing frying oil** repeatedly, oxidises it into polar compounds and aldehydes;
Singapore's Food Agency advises discarding oil once it darkens, smokes or smells off
[@sfaReusingOils]. (Smoke point alone is a poor guide to how well an oil resists heat.)
Steaming, boiling, or stir-frying with less oil is the supported swap.
:::

## Protein: adequacy and quality

Protein supplies amino acids for structure, enzymes, immune molecules and transport. Of the
~20 amino acids, several are **essential** (must come from the diet). A useful population
reference is about **0.8 g/kg/day** — adequate for most healthy adults, though needs rise in
older age, illness and athletic training.

![L-alanine, a representative amino acid: an amino group (–NH₂), a carboxyl group (–COOH), and a variable side chain (here a methyl) that distinguishes one amino acid from another.](/figures/structures/l-alanine.svg){#fig-alanine fig-alt="Skeletal structure of the amino acid L-alanine." width="230"}

**Quality** reflects how well a protein matches human amino-acid needs. Animal proteins are
typically "complete"; most single plant proteins are lower in one or more essential amino
acids but are easily complemented across a day — and plant proteins arrive as a **package
deal** with fibre and unsaturated fat. Protein also has the highest **thermic effect** of
the macronutrients, contributing modestly to energy expenditure and to satiety.

![Tofu — a plant protein that also brings unsaturated fat, with no cholesterol. Photo: yearofeats, CC BY 2.0.](/figures/food/tofu.jpg){#fig-tofu fig-alt="Cubes of fresh tofu." width="320" .lightbox}

::: {.callout-note}
## Food science: heat, browning and protein
Heat **denatures** protein — unfolding it so enzymes can reach it — which usually makes it
*easier* to digest: cooked egg protein is absorbed far better than raw (~91% vs ~51%)
[@evenepoel1998egg]. But the high-heat **Maillard** browning that adds flavour has a
downside: in starchy foods it forms **acrylamide**, a genotoxic carcinogen of concern
[@efsa2015acrylamide], so aim for **golden, not brown** when frying or roasting
[@fsaGoForGold]. Charring meat forms other mutagens, though the human cancer evidence is
inconclusive [@nciCookedMeats] — the practical rule is simply *don't blacken food*.
:::

## From nutrients to patterns

Modern guidelines have moved decisively from "hit this macronutrient ratio" to **"eat these
foods, in this pattern"** [@aha2021diet]. There is **no single ideal macronutrient split**;
guidelines deliberately avoid prescribing one and advise individualising — and referring to
a dietitian where needed [@ada2024care]. The consistent themes are: favour whole grains,
plants and unsaturated fats; minimise added sugar, *trans* fat, sodium and ultra-processed
foods.

## The Singapore picture

Singapore turns these thresholds into everyday tools:

- **My Healthy Plate** — "**quarter, quarter, half**": ¼ wholegrains, ¼ protein, ½ fruit
  and vegetables. A whole-grain quarter is the practical way to act on carbohydrate quality
  [@hpbHealthyPlate].
- **Nutri-Grade** — grades beverages **A–D** by sugar and saturated fat. A live translation
  of the free-sugar cap into a label you can read on a *teh tarik* or bubble tea
  [@hpbNutriGrade].

The reality gap is real. The National Nutrition Survey 2022 found only about **4% of
staples** eaten are wholegrain (against a ~30% recommendation), saturated fat makes up a
large share of fat intake, and most residents exceed the sodium target [@mohNNS2022]. These
numbers are exactly why the thresholds matter — and where a brown-rice or wholemeal swap
earns its keep.

![Brown rice keeps its bran and germ — more fibre and a gentler glucose rise than white rice. Photo: Ceeseven, CC BY-SA 4.0.](/figures/food/brown-rice.jpg){#fig-brown-rice fig-alt="A bowl of cooked brown rice." width="320" .lightbox}

## So — are carbs bad?

Put it together. The question dissolves once you ask *which carbohydrate, from what food?*
A bowl of sugary drink and white rice, and a bowl of brown rice with vegetables and fish,
are both "carbs" — and they are not remotely the same for health. Your first-year job is to
give **quality** guidance ("more wholegrains, fewer free sugars, better fat"), anchor it to
My Healthy Plate, and **refer to a dietitian** when a patient wants a restrictive or
therapeutic diet [@ada2024care]. Resist prescribing grams; that comes later [@crowley2019].

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/macronutrients.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "Doctor, are carbs bad?"

A patient has cut out all rice and bread. Help them replace a slogan with a useful
distinction. Your choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/are-carbs-bad.case.yml"></div>
